coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of chitosan-grafted beta-cyclodextrin for stimuli-responsive drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingwei
Guo, Zhiheng
Xiong, Jianxin
Wu, Datong
Li, Shan
Tao, Yongxin
Qin, Yong
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Modification of natural polysaccharides such as chitosan (CS), β‑cyclodextrin (β-CD), and alginic acid offers a promising strategy for the preparation of smart drug carriers, and latest innovations on such carriers are focused on stimuli-responsive biomaterials. In this study, highly hydrophilic three-demensional (3D) porous CS-grafted β-CD (CS-g-β-CD) was prepared through the Williamson ether synthesis reaction with epichlorohydrin (ECH) as the crosslinker and the consequent nucleophilic reaction between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS, which was then characterized by 1H nuclear (1H NMR), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, scanning electron microscope (SEM), thermogravimetry (TG), and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms. When etoposide (VP16), an anti-cancer drug, was encapsulated in the CS-g-β-CD, the encapsulation ratio was up to 73.6%. Finally, the resultant CS-g-β-CD was successfully used as the responsive drug carrier for pH- and thermo-sensitive release of VP16. This work opens a new avenue for the preparation of stimuli-responsive drug carriers with modified natural polysaccharides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
β-Cyclodextrin
Natural polysaccharides
Epichlorohydrin
Stimuli-responsive drug delivery

ECH-assisted grafting of CS to β-CD
FT-IR spectra of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
XRD analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
Morphologies of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
TG analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
Comparison of CS-g-β-CD, CS and β-CD as drug carrier

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20171194 ), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology Department ( BM2012110 ), Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center ( ACGM2016-06-27 ) and Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the synthesis of ECH-assisted CS-g-β-CD.
1H NMR spectra of ECH, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD.
FT-IR spectra of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD.
XRD patterns of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD.
SEM images of CS (A), β-CD (B), ECH-β-CD (C) and CS-g-β-CD (D). Photographs of CS (E), β-CD (F), ECH-β-CD (G) and CS-g-β-CD (H).
Water drop (3 μL) images and water contact angle measurements on CS (A), β-CD (B) and CS-g-β-CD (C).
TG and DTG analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD in N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (A) and pore size distribution curve (B) of CS-g-β-CD. Temperature, 77 K.
FT-IR spectra (A) and XRD patterns (B) of VP16, CS-g-β-CD and VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD.
Standard curve of the absorbance of VP16 to its concentration.
Schematic illustration showing the encapsulation of VP16 in CS-g-β-CD.
(A) Kinetic release profiles of VP16 from VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD in 0.1 M PBS of pH 4.5, 5.8 and 7.4 at 37 °C. (B) Schematic illustration showing the pH-sensitive release of VP16.
Kinetic release profiles of VP16 from VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 at 25, 37, and 42 °C.
Kinetic release profiles of VP16 from VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD, CS and β-CD in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 at 37 °C.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of chitosan-grafted beta-cyclodextrin for stimuli-responsive drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gynecology and Obstetrics, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pneumology Department, Changzhou Children's Hospital, Changzhou 213003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modification of natural polysaccharides such as chitosan (CS), β‑cyclodextrin (β-CD), and alginic acid offers a promising strategy for the preparation of smart drug carriers, and latest innovations on such carriers are focused on stimuli-responsive biomaterials. In this study, highly hydrophilic three-demensional (3D) porous CS-grafted β-CD (CS-g-β-CD) was prepared through the Williamson ether synthesis reaction with epichlorohydrin (ECH) as the crosslinker and the consequent nucleophilic reaction between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS, which was then characterized by 1H nuclear (1H NMR), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, scanning electron microscope (SEM), thermogravimetry (TG), and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms. When etoposide (VP16), an anti-cancer drug, was encapsulated in the CS-g-β-CD, the encapsulation ratio was up to 73.6%. Finally, the resultant CS-g-β-CD was successfully used as the responsive drug carrier for pH- and thermo-sensitive release of VP16. This work opens a new avenue for the preparation of stimuli-responsive drug carriers with modified natural polysaccharides.

ce:section.ce:para: With increasing concerns on tumor therapy while minimizing the side effects of anti-cancer drugs, the development of advanced materials as smart drug carriers has become an urgent task [1–3]. Although nanoparticles [4], synthetic polymers [5], and mesoporous materials [6] have been used as drug carriers, natural polysaccharides have significant advantages over these materials due to their renewable resources, non-toxicity and biocompatibility [7–10]. However, the use of natural polysaccharides in drug delivery systems is limited by their low solubility in acid-free aqueous media [11], and thus the modification of natural polysaccharides is of great importance for the efficient utilization of them. For example, chitosan/gelatin modified with acetylsalicylic acid via electrophoretic deposition in ethanol exhibits an improved hydrophilicity, which can be used for the sustained-release of acetylsalicylic acid [12]. A highly hydrophilic poly (3‑hydroxybutyrate‑co‑3‑hydroxyvalerate) (PHBV) membrane for long-term sustained drug delivery is obtained by introducing cellulose nanocrystals as the nanofillers [13]. Although the past decades have witnessed the achievements in polysaccharides modification for medical applications, it is still a challenging and urgent task for the facile preparation of hydrophilic polysaccharides-based biomaterials for controlled drug release.

Herein, we report on the facile covalent grafting of chitosan (CS) to β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) under mild conditions through the Williamson ether synthesis reaction with epichlorohydrin (ECH) as the crosslinker and the consequent nucleophilic reaction between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS. Compared with CS and β-CD, the resultant CS-grafted β-CD (CS-g-β-CD) exhibits a remarkably enhanced hydrophilicity since the three-demensional (3D) porous structure of the CS-g-β-CD is advantageous to the full exposure of the polar functional groups (amino groups and hydroxyl groups) to the surface. Next, the CS-g-β-CD is fully characterized and used as the responsive drug carrier for pH- and thermo-sensitive release of etoposide (VP16), an anti-cancer drug, and the possible mechanisms of controlled release of VP16 from the CS-g-β-CD carrier are also discussed in this work.

Chitosan (CS, degree of deacetylation 95%) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD), epichlorohydrin (ECH) and etoposide (VP16) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals not mentioned were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of 18.2 MΩ (Milli-Q, Millipore).

1H NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker Avance 500 MHz (1H frequency) spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a D/max 2500PC X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The morphologies of different samples were characterized on a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss, Germany). The hydrophilicity of different samples was evaluated by water contact angle measurements on a DSA25 water contact angle goniometer (Kruss GmbH, Germany), in which a water droplet of 3 μL was dropped onto the testing surface. Thermogravimetry (TG) and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) analysis was performed on a TG 209 F3 thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany) in N2 atmosphere, and the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore size distribution were determined by using an ASAP 2010 specific surface area and pore size analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). A model U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used for the determination of VP16 concentration according to the standard curve predetermined. The temperatures for drug release were controlled with a model DKB-501A thermostatic bath (Shanghai Jinghong Laboratory Instrument Co. Ltd., China).

2.5 g of β-CD was dissolved in 100 mL of 1 M NaOH and magnetically stirred for 8 h, and then 10 mL of ECH was added into the β-CD solution and continuously stirred at room temperature for 4 h. After the excessive ECH was thoroughly extracted with ethyl acetate, the separated water phase was transferred to a rotary evaporator to evaporate the solvent at 60 °C under vacuum, and 8.6 mL of ECH modified β-CD (ECH-β-CD) was obtained. 1 H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO‑ d6, ppm): δ = 4.78–4.76 (t, 3H), 4.55–4.54 (d, 3H), 4.47–4.45 (t, 5H), 3.67–3.62 (m, 8H), 3.38–3.28 (m, 22H), 2.86–2.84 (t, 2H), 2.71–2.69 (m, 2H). The obtained ECH-β-CD was dissolved in 100 mL of water

Next, 0.5 g of CS was dissolved in 50 mL of 0.1 M HCl and continuously stirred for 12 h, which was then added to the above ECH-β-CD solution. 1 M NaOH was used to adjust the resultant solution to weak alkalinity (pH ~8.0), and then the mixture was magnetically stirred for 1.5 h at 60 °C. The resultant CS-g-β-CD hydrogels were thoroughly washed with water and freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C for 24 h. 1H NMR (500 MHz, DMSO‑ d6, ppm): δ = 6.25–5.48 (m, 6H), 5.13–5.08 (m, 4H), 4.61–4.57 (t, 2H), 3.80–3.71 (t, 2H), 3.47–3.36 (m, 1H), 2.71–2.68 (m, 2H).

5 mg of VP16 was dissolved in 100 mL of water, and then 100 mg of the CS-g-β-CD was added to the above solution. After magnetic stirring at 37 °C for 24 h, the VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD was centrifugally separated and subsequently freeze-dried at −40 °C for 24 h. The residual VP16 in the supernatant was calculated from the UV–vis spectra at the selected wavelength of 285 nm [14,15], and the encapsulation ratio of VP16 was determined by the following equation:
(1)
Encapsulation ratio
%
=
1 −
w r
/
w a
× 100
where w a (mg) and w r (mg) represent VP16 added to the solution and remained in the supernatant after encapsulation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH- and thermo-sensitive release of VP16
ce:section.ce:para: 30 mg of the VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD was filled into a dialysis bag having a molecular weight cut-off of 1000, and then it was immersed into 30 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS) of pH 5.8, 6.5 and 7.4, respectively. The pH-sensitive release of VP16 was carried out at 37 °C with continuous stirring. To quantify the amount of VP16 released from the CS-g-β-CD, 3 mL of the PBS was taken out every 30 min for the UV analysis at λ max = 285 nm and an equal volume of fresh PBS was added to the solution for replenishment. The cumulative release of VP16 at time t was determined by dividing the total amount of VP16 released at time t by the encapsulated amount of VP16 in the CS-g-β-CD.

To investigate the thermo-sensitive release of VP16, the dialysis bag with VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD was immersed into 30 mL of 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4. Next, the thermo-sensitive release of VP16 was studied at 25 °C, 37 °C, and 42 °C, respectively, with continuous stirring. Other operations and the calculation of cumulative release were the same as those described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECH-assisted grafting of CS to β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: ECH-assisted grafting of CS to β-CD is achieved via two successive reactions, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . First, the ECH crosslinker is tethered to β-CD via the Williamson ether synthesis reaction [16] to produce ECH-β-CD. Next, a nucleophilic reaction occurs between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS under alkaline conditions to produce a secondary amine (NH) and a hydroxyl group (OH) [17], and this ring-opening reaction results in the formation of CS-grafted β-CD (CS-g-β-CD).

The successful grafting of CS to β-CD can be verified from the 1H NMR spectra of ECH, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD (Fig. 2 ). For ECH, the signal at 3.57 ppm is attributable to the CH2 Cl (Ha), and a downfield shift of this signal is observed on the 1H NMR spectrum of ECH-β-CD (Ha, 3.67–3.62 ppm), which can be ascribed to the change from CH2 Cl to CH2 O via the Williamson ether synthesis reaction [18]. For ECH and ECH-β-CD, the two signals in the δ 2.92–2.88 and 2.71–2.68 region are assigned to the hydrogens on the epoxide ring of ECH (Hc), however, only one signal is observed in the δ 2.71–2.68 ppm region on the spectrum of CS-g-β-CD, which is due to the methylene hydrogens of NHCH2 (Hc). In addition, a broad peak in the δ 6.25–5.48 ppm region on the spectrum of CS-g-β-CD corresponds to the hydrogens of the NH2 and NH groups (Hd) [19], suggesting that CS is successfully grafted to β-CD since the NH2 and NH groups come from CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectra of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD are shown in Fig. 3 . For β-CD, the broad peak centered at 3373 cm−1 is assigned to OH stretching vibration of pure β-CD crystal, and another two peaks at 1421 and 1021 cm−1 are attributed to OH bending vibration of pure β-CD and CO stretching vibration in sugar rings of pure β-CD, respectively [20]. The FT-IR spectrum of ECH-β-CD is similar to that of β-CD, indicating that the framework of β-CD is not changed after the modification with ECH [21], however, two new peaks at 1200 and 1077 cm−1 are observed for ECH-β-CD, which are due to COC stretching vibration of epoxide ring and the ether stretching vibration, respectively [22]. For CS, the two characteristic peaks located at 1602 and 894 cm−1 are due to NH in-plane and out-of-plane bending vibrations, respectively [23], and the two peaks can still be observed on the FT-IR spectrum of CS-g-β-CD, implying that CS is grafted to β-CD. In addition, a shoulder at 748 cm−1 is observed for the CS-g-β-CD, which is attributable to the CH2 rocking vibration within the NHCH2 CHOH bridge [24]. This result further confirms that the grafting of CS to the ECH-β-CD is achieved via the nucleophilic reaction between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD. CS exhibits two major peaks at 10.98° and 20.14°, which are assigned to Form I and Form II crystals of CS [25–27]. The crystal of β-CD stacks mainly in the cage-type structure with sharp diffraction peaks in the range of 10°–30° [20]. While these characteristic peaks aroused from β-CD almost disappear in the CS-g-β-CD, meanwhile, the peak intensity of CS is remarkably decreased in the CS-g-β-CD. The disappearance of the diffraction peaks of β-CD and the decreased crystallinity of CS suggest the formation of CS-g-β-CD [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD are shown in Fig. 5A–D. Compared with CS, β-CD and ECH-β-CD (Fig. 5A–C), the CS-g-β-CD exhibits a 3D porous network structure and the size of cavities is ~1 μm (Fig. 5D), which is especially beneficial to the encapsulation of drug molecules. Fig. 5E–H show the photographs of CS, β-CD, ECH-β-CD and CS-g-β-CD. As can be seen, the white powders of β-CD (Fig. 5F) are changed into a light yellow liquid (Fig. 5G) after the modification with colorless ECH, and then the light yellow liquid of ECH-β-CD is further changed into a white hydrogel (Fig. 5H) after the grafting of CS (Fig. 5E) to the ECH-β-CD under alkaline conditions.

Because the formation of 3D porous network structure within the CS-g-β-CD, it is expected that more polar functional groups (amino groups and hydroxyl groups) are exposed to the surface of CS-g-β-CD than CS and β-CD. This hypothesis is further confirmed by the wettability measurements. As shown in Fig. 6 , the water contact angles on CS and β-CD are 110° and 74°, respectively. The hydrophilicity of CS-g-β-CD is significantly enhanced with a decreased water contact angle of 22°. Obviously, the remarkably enhanced hydrophilicity can be ascribed to the fact that the 3D porous network structure of CS-g-β-CD is advantageous to the full exposure of the polar groups to the surface of CS-g-β-CD. Thus, the ECH-assisted grafting of CS to β-CD can convert hydrophobic surfaces into hydrophilic surfaces, which is beneficial for the solubility enhancement of CS and β-CD and the efficient utilization of them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TG analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD
ce:section.ce:para: TG and DTG analysis of CS, β-CD and CS-g-β-CD was carried out at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in flowing nitrogen to investigate the decomposition course. As shown in Fig. 7 , the weight loss around 50–120 °C is assigned to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water on the three samples, and the significant weight loss at around 300 °C is due to the rapid decomposition of CS [29] and β-CD [30]. Also in Fig. 7, a sharp DTG peak at around 300 °C is observed for CS-g-β-CD, and the DTG curves of CS and β-CD are omitted in this figure since the DTG peaks of CS and β-CD are located at the same position as that of CS-g-β-CD. The remaining weight is about 16% and 4% for CS and β-CD, respectively, at the end of the TG experiment (950 °C), however, the content of CS-g-β-CD is estimated to be 23% at 950 °C. The observed trend indicates that the CS-g-β-CD has greater thermal stability over CS and β-CD, which might be inferred due to the formed 3D porous network structure within the CS-g-β-CD.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the CS-g-β-CD are studied to understand its structure features. According to the IUPAC classification, the CS-g-β-CD exhibits a type IV isotherm (Fig. 8A). The BET analysis shows that the specific surface area of CS-g-β-CD is 23.0 m2 g−1, which is greatly larger than those of CS (0.06 m2 g−1) and β-CD (0.62 m2 g−1), and the greatly enhanced specific area of CS-g-β-CD can be ascribed to its 3D porous network structure. The pore size distribution curve shows that the average pore size of CS-g-β-CD is 1.995 nm (Fig. 8B), further confirming the microporous structure of the CS-g-β-CD revealed by the SEM results (Fig. 5D). Noted that the molecular length of VP16 is about 1.34 nm [31], which is smaller than the average pore size of CS-g-β-CD (1.995 nm). That is to say, the molecule of VP16 can be encapsulated into the CS-g-β-CD.

The cumulative release profiles of VP16 from the VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD in 0.1 M PBS of different pH values at 37 °C are shown in Fig. 12A. For all the tested pH values (4.5, 5.8 and 7.4), a quick release occurs in the first 3 h, and then the release of VP16 is gradually slow down. The cumulative release of VP16 at pH 4.5, 5.8 and 7.4 is determined to be 90.0%, 81.4% and 70.1%, respectively, when the release of VP 16 is performed for 10 h. The pH-sensitive release of VP16 is closely related to the ionization state of CS in the CS-g-β-CD. The acid dissociation constant (pK a) of CS is ~6.5 [35], and therefore the amino groups of CS are protonated and positively charged (NH3 +) at pH 4.5 and 5.8 while maintain the neutral form (NH2) at pH 7.4. The pH-sensitive release of VP16 can be illustrated in Fig. 12B. At pH of 5.8 and 4.4, the electrostatic repulsion between the positively charged CS chains would unfold the interwoven CS chains, resulting in the release of more VP16 than pH 7.4. When the pH is decreased from 5.8 to 4.4, the amount of VP16 released is further increased by the strengthened electrostatic repulsion. Noted that this result is of particular importance for cancer patients, because the microenvironment in tumor tissues is usually characteristically acidic and the local pH is decreased to the range of 5.5–7.0 owing to the insufficient blood perfusion, hypoxia and glycolytic cell metabolism [36,37].

The release of VP16 from the VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD is also investigated at different temperatures. Fig. 13 shows the cumulative release profiles of VP16 in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 at 25, 37 and 42 °C, respectively. The release rate of VP16 is significantly increased with increasing temperature. The cumulative release of VP16 at 25, 37 and 42 °C is calculated to be 41.9%, 70.1% and 86.7%, respectively, when the release of VP 16 is carried out for 10 h. The thermo-sensitive release of VP 16 can be ascribed to the thermo-sensitive hydrogen bonds between VP16 and the CS-g-β-CD. Since the CS-g-β-CD possesses amounts of amino groups and hydroxyl groups, hydrogen bond interactions play a dominant role in the combination process between the VP16 molecule containing hydroxyl groups and the CS-g-β-CD carrier. When the VP16 molecule is released from the CS-g-β-CD carrier, it must absorb heat from the environment to destroy the hydrogen bond. Therefore, the amount of VP16 released from the CS-g-β-CD is increased with increasing temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CS-g-β-CD, CS and β-CD as drug carrier
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 depicts the release of VP16 from VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD, CS and β-CD in 0.1 M PBS of pH 7.4 at 37 °C. The encapsulation and release of VP16 from CS and β-CD are carried out under the same conditions as those of CS-g-β-CD. A burst-release of VP16 is observed for CS and β-CD, and the cumulative release of VP16 reaches as high as 95.2% and 84.9%, respectively, in the first 1 h. Fortunately, the burst-release behavior of VP16 is circumvented in the CS-g-β-CD carrier, since the cumulative release of VP16 from the CS-g-β-CD is only 33.4% in the same time period. When the release is carried out for 10 h, the encapsulated VP16 is almost completely released from CS and β-CD, whereas the cumulative release of VP16 from the CS-g-β-CD is only 70.1%. Obviously, the sustained-release of VP16 from the CS-g-β-CD can be ascribed to the 3D porous network structure of CS-g-β-CD, which reduces the permeability of the drug carrier.

To summarize, highly hydrophilic CS-g-β-CD with a 3D porous network structure was prepared through the Williamson ether synthesis reaction with \\172.20.145.95\user-work\GC32\BIOMAC\BIOMAC_11309\AppData\Local\Youdao\Dict\Application\7.5.2.0\resultui\dict\ECH as the crosslinker and the consequent nucleophilic reaction between the epoxide ring of ECH and the primary amine of CS. The as-prepared CS-g-β-CD was fully characterized and used as the carrier of VP16, an anti-cancer drug. The kinetic release behaviors of VP16 demonstrate that the release of VP16 from the VP16 encapsulated CS-g-β-CD is pH- and thermo-sensitive. Compared with CS and β-CD, the sustained-release of VP16 from the CS-g-β-CD carrier could be ascribed to the 3D porous network structure of CS-g-β-CD, which reduced the permeability of the drug delivery system. These findings of the work are beneficial for the synthesis of multi-responsive drug carriers based on modified natural polysaccharides, opening a new avenue for controlled drug delivery in the future
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171194), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology Department (BM2012110), Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center (ACGM2016-06-27) and Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
